questions.question: Explain the three main layers of Clean Architecture in Android.
keyPoints: Presentation Layer - handles UI and user interactions
Domain Layer - contains business logic and use cases
Data Layer - manages data sources and repositories
Dependencies flow inward toward domain layer

questions.question: What are the advantages of using MVVM architecture over MVC in Android?
keyPoints: MVVM provides better separation of concerns
ViewModel survives configuration changes
LiveData enables reactive UI updates
Easier testing due to decoupled ViewModel
Reduces boilerplate code with data binding

questions.question: Describe the role of Repository pattern in Android architecture.
keyPoints: Provides abstraction over data sources
Single source of truth for data
Handles data from multiple sources (local/remote)
Decouples data layer from rest of application
Simplifies data access for ViewModels

questions.question: What is Dependency Injection and why is it important in Android architecture?
keyPoints: Design pattern where dependencies are provided externally
Improves code modularity and reusability
Makes testing easier by allowing mock dependencies
Common tools: Hilt, Dagger, Koin
Reduces tight coupling between components

questions.question: Explain the difference between ViewModel and LiveData in Android Architecture Components.
keyPoints: ViewModel - holds UI-related data, survives configuration changes
LiveData - observable data holder that notifies observers
ViewModel is lifecycle-aware but not observable by itself
LiveData can be observed for changes
Both work together in MVVM pattern

questions.question: What are the key components of Android Architecture Components?
keyPoints: ViewModel - manages UI state
LiveData - observable data holder
Room - SQLite database abstraction
Navigation - fragment navigation
Data Binding - connects UI to data sources
Lifecycle - handles component lifecycle

questions.question: Explain why separation of concerns is important in Android application design.
keyPoints: Makes code more maintainable
Enables easier testing of individual components
Allows parallel development by different teams
Reduces code duplication
Makes application scalable and extendable

questions.question: Compare MVC, MVP, and MVVM architecture patterns in Android. Which would you recommend for a large-scale application and why?
keyPoints: MVC: Model-View-Controller with Controller handling user input
MVP: Presenter acts as intermediary between View and Model
MVVM: ViewModel binds data to View automatically
For large apps: MVVM or Clean Architecture recommended
MVVM provides better lifecycle handling and testability
Clean Architecture offers best separation for complex apps
Clean Architecture has independent layers that can be tested separately

questions.question: Design a Clean Architecture structure for a login feature in an Android application. Explain each layer and its components.
keyPoints: Presentation: Activity/Fragment, ViewModel, UI state
Domain: UseCase for login validation, Entity for user
Data: Repository implementation, API service, local storage
Data Sources: Remote (API) and Local (SharedPreferences/Room)
Dependency flow: Presentation depends on Domain, Domain has no dependencies
Each layer communicates through interfaces
Use Cases encapsulate business rules

questions.question: Explain how you would implement offline-first architecture in an Android app using Room database and Repository pattern.
keyPoints: Repository checks local data first (Room DB)
If local data exists, return it immediately
In background, sync with remote API
Update local database with fresh data
Notify UI of changes through LiveData/Flow
Handle network states (loading, success, error)
Use WorkManager for background sync
Design: Repository -> UseCase -> ViewModel -> UI

questions.question: Discuss the lifecycle of Android components and how Architecture Components help manage state across configuration changes.
keyPoints: Activities and Fragments destroyed on configuration changes
ViewModel survives and retains data
LiveData observes lifecycle and stops updates when inactive
Saved state handle preserves small amounts of data
ViewModelScope manages coroutines in ViewModel
Proper lifecycle awareness prevents memory leaks
ViewModelProvider creates ViewModels scoped to Activity/Fragment
